Fact: The temperature danger zone for TCS food is between 41°F and 135°F.
TCS food must be cooled from 135°F to 70°F within 2 hours.
After reaching 70°F food must continue cooling to 41°F within 4 more hours.
The total cooling window for TCS food is 6 hours from 135°F to 41°F.
Hot TCS food must be held at 135°F or above at all times.
Cold TCS food must be held at 41°F or below at all times.
Poultry must reach a minimum internal cooking temperature of 165°F for 15 seconds.
Ground meat must reach a minimum internal cooking temperature of 155°F for 15 seconds.
Whole muscle beef pork and seafood must reach 145°F for 15 seconds.
All TCS food reheated for service must reach 165°F for 15 seconds.
Food reheated in a microwave must reach 165°F and rest covered for 2 minutes.
Stuffed meats and stuffed poultry must reach 165°F because the stuffing contacts raw proteins.
Ready-to-eat TCS food held at 41°F or below may be kept for a maximum of 7 days.
TCS food that has been in the danger zone for more than 4 cumulative hours must be discarded.
Shallow pans ice baths and blast chillers are all approved methods for rapid cooling.
Cross-contamination occurs when pathogens are transferred from one food or surface to another.
Raw poultry must always be stored on the lowest shelf in a cooler.
Foods in a cooler are stored from top to bottom by minimum required cooking temperature.
Ready-to-eat food must always be stored above all raw proteins in a refrigerator.
Cross-contact refers specifically to the transfer of allergen proteins from one food to another.
Even a trace amount of an allergen can trigger a life-threatening reaction in a sensitive individual.
The nine major food allergens are milk eggs fish shellfish tree nuts peanuts wheat soybeans and sesame.
Dedicated color-coded cutting boards help prevent cross-contamination between different food types.
Gloves must be changed and hands must be washed when switching between raw and ready-to-eat foods.
Food-contact surfaces must be cleaned and sanitized immediately after contact with a raw protein.
Allergen-free meals must be prepared first or on completely separate equipment to prevent cross-contact.
A food slicer used continuously must be cleaned and sanitized every 4 hours.
Chemicals must always be stored in original labeled containers away from all food and equipment.
Food handlers must wash their hands for a minimum of 20 seconds using soap and warm water.
Hands must be washed after using the restroom handling raw meat touching the face and handling garbage.
Hand sanitizer may only be used after proper handwashing — it is a supplement not a substitute.
Handwashing must occur at a designated handwashing sink only — never in a prep or dish sink.
Water for handwashing must be at a minimum temperature of 100°F.
Hands must be dried with a single-use paper towel or air dryer after washing.
A plain band ring is the only jewelry permitted for food handlers.
Fingernails must be kept short clean and unpolished to prevent harboring pathogens.
A food handler with vomiting or diarrhea must be excluded from the operation immediately.
The five reportable illnesses in food service are Norovirus Hepatitis A Salmonella Typhi Shigella and E. coli O157:H7.
Jaundice is a symptom of Hepatitis A and requires immediate exclusion and reporting to the regulatory authority.
An infected wound on the hand must be covered with a bandage and protected with a single-use glove.
Food handlers may drink in food prep areas only from a covered container with a straw.
Cold TCS food must be received at 41°F or below.
Shell eggs and shucked shellfish may be received at 45°F or below.
Live shellfish must be received at an air temperature of 45°F or below.
Hot TCS food received during a hot delivery must arrive at 135°F or above.
Frozen food must be received completely solid with no signs of thawing or refreezing.
Dented swollen rusted or leaking canned goods must always be rejected at receiving.
Shellfish tags must be kept on file for 90 days from the date the last shellfish was sold.
The FIFO method — First In First Out — ensures older stock is used before newer stock.
Dry goods must be stored at least 6 inches off the floor to allow cleaning and deter pests.
Raw chicken must be stored on the lowest shelf of any refrigerator or walk-in cooler.
Ready-to-eat fruit and cooked food must be stored on the top shelf above all raw proteins.
Large ice crystals on frozen food indicate it thawed and refroze during transport and must be rejected.
Refrigerators holding TCS food must maintain a temperature of 41°F or below at all times.
A delivery that arrives with tampered or broken packaging must be rejected regardless of temperature.
The four approved thawing methods are refrigeration running cold water microwave and cooking from frozen.
Food thawed in a microwave must be cooked immediately after thawing.
Food thawed under running water must be kept under water at 70°F or below.
Produce must be washed under cool running water before cutting peeling or cooking.
Ice must always be handled with a scoop — never with bare hands or a glass.
Separate utensils must be used for raw proteins and ready-to-eat foods to prevent cross-contamination.
Food contaminated by chemicals glass or other physical hazards must be discarded immediately.
Partial cooking must be limited to no more than 60 minutes before the food is finished cooking.
Whole muscle beef roasts may be cooked to 145°F but must be held at that temperature for 4 minutes.
Time-temperature abused food must be discarded — reheating or chilling does not make it safe again.
Cleaning removes dirt and organic matter while sanitizing reduces pathogens to safe levels.
A surface must always be cleaned before it can be effectively sanitized.
The correct order for a three-compartment sink is wash rinse sanitize and air-dry.
The wash compartment of a three-compartment sink must be at least 110°F.
Chlorine sanitizer must be maintained between 50 and 99 ppm to be effective and safe.
Iodine sanitizer must be maintained between 12.5 and 25 ppm.
Quaternary ammonium sanitizer concentration must follow the manufacturer's instructions.
Food-contact surfaces in continuous use must be cleaned and sanitized at least every 4 hours.
Sanitized equipment must always be allowed to air-dry — never dried with a towel.
A high-temperature dish machine must reach 180°F on the surface of dishes during the final rinse.
A low-temperature dish machine relies on chemical sanitizer and requires a minimum rinse of 120°F.
Wiping cloths must be stored in sanitizing solution between uses — never in plain water.
Test strips must be used to verify sanitizer concentration — always check the strip expiration date first.
A cutting board with deep knife scars cannot be effectively sanitized and must be replaced.
Ice machines must be cleaned regularly per manufacturer instructions to prevent mold and bacterial buildup.
Handwashing sinks must be used for handwashing only — never for food prep or equipment rinsing.
Every handwashing sink must be stocked with soap single-use towels and a waste bin at all times.
A handwashing sink must provide running water at a minimum temperature of 100°F.
A handwashing reminder sign must be posted at every handwashing sink.
Floor-mounted equipment must be elevated at least 6 inches off the floor to allow cleaning underneath.
Light fixtures in food prep areas must have shatter-resistant covers to prevent glass contamination.
Adequate lighting in a walk-in cooler allows staff to read labels check dates and identify spoilage.
A backed-up drain in a food prep area is an immediate health hazard — food prep must stop.
Garbage containers in food prep areas must have tight-fitting lids to deter pests.
A backflow prevention device stops contaminated water from reversing into the potable water supply.
The most effective pest prevention strategy is eliminating food water and shelter sources.
All gaps cracks and openings in exterior walls floors and doors must be sealed to prevent pest entry.
Self-closing doors and door sweeps work together to prevent pests from entering through exterior doors.
Window screens must be tight-fitting and in good repair in any food prep area with operable windows.
Droppings nesting materials and gnaw marks are the most common early signs of a pest infestation.
Only a licensed pest control operator may apply pesticides in a food service facility.
Cardboard boxes provide ideal harborage for cockroaches and must be removed from the facility promptly.
Cockroach infestations often produce a distinctive musty oily odor in addition to visible droppings.
Rodents can enter through a gap as small as a dime — all openings must be sealed regardless of size.
Fruit flies breed in floor drains and drain pans — keeping these clean eliminates their breeding sites.
Fly light traps must be positioned away from food to prevent dead insects from falling into food.
Pest control inspection reports must be reviewed after each visit and findings acted upon promptly.
The person in charge must be present and knowledgeable about food safety at all times during operation.
Active managerial control means identifying and correcting food safety risks before they cause illness.
HACCP stands for Hazard Analysis Critical Control Points.
The three types of food safety hazards are biological chemical and physical.
A critical control point is a step in food handling where a hazard can be prevented eliminated or reduced.
When a critical control point is not met corrective action must be taken immediately.
Food safety records provide written proof that monitoring and corrective actions were performed.
A food recall requires the immediate removal of affected product from service.
Multiple employees reporting stomach illness on the same day may indicate a foodborne illness outbreak.
The difference between restriction and exclusion is that restriction limits duties while exclusion removes the food handler entirely.
Foodborne illness affects an estimated 48 million Americans every year.
The top five risk factors for foodborne illness are improper holding temperatures inadequate cooking poor personal hygiene contaminated equipment and unsafe food sources.
Most foodborne illnesses are preventable with consistent application of basic food safety practices.
Norovirus is the most common cause of foodborne illness in the United States.
Bacteria multiply most rapidly between 70°F and 125°F — the middle of the temperature danger zone.
Under ideal conditions a single bacterium can multiply to over one million in just 10 hours.
TCS stands for Temperature Control for Safety.
The ServSafe exam consists of 90 questions and requires a score of 75% or higher to pass.
A food safety culture starts with management — when managers take food safety seriously staff follow.
When in doubt throw it out — the cost of discarding food is always less than the cost of a foodborne illness outbreak.
